commentBody: Fun Saturday!

That was a very easy puzzle 
that for some reason 
took quite a while to solve!

Nice one, Katie!
Woohoo! A Friday and Saturday, back to back, that were fun to do. Thank you. It was looking tough on the entire right half of the puzzle. I’m very satisfied.

A very tasty Saturday puzzle.  I was practically LOLing out loud at the wittiness of the clues.  [List of qualifications?] KINDASORTAMAYBE, indeed.  And, I loved the mini food theme, ALOO, PANEER, STRAWBERRYSHAKE, and STEAKSALAD.  I love cooking Indian food, but haven’t mastered parathas yet.

[Gold meddlers?] for ALCHEMISTS was priceless, and the central [List of qualifications?] was clever, too.

19D was a sad reminder for our Philly solvers tonight, a little dig Embiided into the puzzle.

I think the editors got confused and switched the Friday and Saturday this week. Maybe it's just me but I thought yesterday's puzzle was the hardest Friday in a long time but today's was a breeze. 

But anyway, this was a fantastic Saturday! Loved the clues.
Thank you, "Squid Game" for helping me with 52D.

Tough one with lots of clever clues. 60D was a personal, well professional, gimme.

Half a lifetime ago (literally) when I was working at my father's engineering firm, he decided he needed a LEED AP on staff to help get some contracts where the client was interested in pursuing LEED benefits.

I was selected to take the test, not because I knew the most about the topic, but because I was the best test taker in the office. It was a tough test. There's a whole point system for LEED certification, and the test was closed book. I had to memorize the entire point system. I spent a few weeks memorizing it. I found it very curious that the test was about the part that would be the easiest to look up when it came to actual projects, rather than applied knowledge that needed to be learned through experience

It wasn't the toughest puzzle I've ever done, but I thought a lot of it was really witty.  

I love BABAR for [Weighty subject of some children's books], the [List of qualifications?] was absolutely brilliant, and I am very grateful that it jumped out at me when I had about half the crosses.  

I was embarrassed at how long it took me to get 33A. D'oh!

15A was, IMHO (I could be wrong) definitely a Saturday level clue, pointing out as it does our evanescence (love that word--it seems to do what it describes).  

Love me some ROOF TOP BARS, although the wagon I'm on is pretty scary on a roof top.

I think Barry has it right--it was a relatively easy puzzle, but it really had some pop to it.

Can anyone tell me why KILO is followed by LIMA?  

Is that a thousand capitals of Peru?  A thousand beans of a certain type?

Terry Gross has the same number of letters as Ari Shapiro. Otherwise I'd have been here sooner.  1912, what a year! Oreos, which debuted that year, are good for dunking. The Titanic, which also debuted in 1912, did not do as well with dunking. (And it is good to see mention of the greatest dunker of all time -- IMHO -- Dr. J.) Also liked alchemists atop mere mortals. Gold meddlers was fun, I thought about prospectors, smelters, jewelers and Olympic cheaters from East Germany and the USSR (and Russia at Sochi), but alchemists takes the cake. 

I also like the cross of BABAR and ARF; which melded in my mind to form the nauseous elephantine portmanteau, BABARF. Which is what I would do after having a steak salad and strawberry shake. I really would. I'd be like...BABARF!!!! 

List of qualifications and bath seat are awesome clues. Katie Hoody is a great favorite here and I can see why. At least two of her puzzles were nominated and did quite well in the voting for The Griddies last year. Great to see her back.
A relief today after Friday to discover one had not simply forgotten how to do crosswords.

[Kilo follower] took a while – gram before watt before LIMA. Aha!

[Weighty subject of some children's books] had me putting in death before grief before BABAR. Aha! (And a big smile.)

And ARFS might have been KINDASORTAMAYBE meh-inducing were it not for the charming clue [Husky hellos] so I wouldn't worry about it, Katie. It was swell and also elicited an, you guessed it, aha!

The whole puzzle was chock-full of ahas, which made for a most delicious romp, as if I'd just dined on a juicy STEAK (with) SALAD and followed it with a yummy STRAWBERRY SHAKE.
 
Thank you, Katie –
I feel wonderfully satisfied!

This ditty was percolating in my brain today while in the garden, so I’ll use the excuse of “spring up=EMERGE” to foist it on you:

When the kitchen shears are in the second drawer,
And middle May arrives to cheers,
Then peas will guide the planting, 
And greeeeeen will be the spears!

This is the dawning of the age of asparagus,
Age of asparagus,
Asparagus,
Aspaaaaaragus

Olive oil and pan sautéing,
Hollandaise a must, abounding.
No more frozen stuff from last year, 
Golden living dreams of fresh spears,
Mystic mythic vegetation
And the palate’s liberation,
Asparagus, Asparagus.

OK then - back to puzzling.

"I have to dogsit this weekend."
"Here, let me give you some pointers."

("Oh, thanks! You're such a great collie-gue.")

Won land, Gold meddlers, Husky hellos, No time like the present, A zero is one, Bath seat, Portable retirement option, Where spirits are high, plus a couple of Central points and Parathas... There was a lot of truly inspired clueing in this one, every couple of minutes I had an "Oh that's great!" moment!
Don't care what day it is, a great crossword is a great crossword.
Top job Katie, thanks!

Re: the mini.
It’s a small thing, but it honestly, an animation after a solve makes my day every time. It's the perfect "job well done" before I head back to reality!

Wow, Katie Hoody has had some solid puzzles but this was one for the ages. I’m surprised people found this easier than yesterday because it took me about 50% longer. Not sure I would have ever found an in if I hadn’t come up with WENTONTOUR and SCARYMOVIE on my own. I can’t find one flaw in this grid, and I can’t find it a single clue that wasn’t well thought out or phoned in. Even OREO for Pete’s sake, I was looking for an early movie title. Any of the clues you thought would be gimmes weren’t—COT instead of IRA, UMPS before BIRD (my fault not noticing it wasn’t plural), EVEN before OVAL, BYTE before LIMA, it just goes on. Folks when we say that a lot of what makes a Saturday a Saturday is not the grid but the clues, this is what we mean. And the longer entries were also clued brilliantly one after another. Just look at the central spanners and all those brilliantly clued stacks. I’m not going to list my favorite clues because I don’t have enough space and I would hate to leave any out.
Between yesterday and today I have hope that the weekend themeless solve is getting back to its former glory.
Excellent puzzle. So many top-notch clues. Well done!

So frustrating -- at first -- and so enjoyable by the time I filled in the last letter.  I loved the alchemists clue and had a good laugh after realizing the Scary Movie clue was not a mistake. This puzzle was so good I'll even forgive the constructor for including the accursed Oreo in the fill.
Started out slowly but 28D got things going and helped fill in the SW corner first. All the long entries were so much fun. Enjoyable and challenging Saturday. Thank you, Katie!

Some people are saying that Friday and Saturday should have been switched, but I finished both in about the same amount of time, around my Friday average. After my initial runthrough, (MOE, ARIL, ALOO, STRATI, and a few more) it took me a while to get onto Katie's wave length. As a kid, I always got a STRAWBERRY SHAKE at McDonald's. They were so thick you really couldn't use a straw to drink one. (Mind you, I'm not saying they were healthy.)
I usually just barely look at the constructor's notes, but I read the whole thing tonight. Katie gives a shout out to Robyn Weintraub. It was nice to see.
This one was a toughie for me - lots of resistance and then sudden rays of sunlight, to mix my metaphors.  But huge fun. Some groans and hahas as answers revealed themselves. Thank you!

Very enjoyable puzzle with delicious clueing, yet surprisingly easy to crack. Close to a personal best at 13:31. 

Didn’t know DABO, PAWNEE, AMYS and SKOR, but deduced them easily from crosses. Loved: Gold meddlers, Husky hellos, Portable retirement options, List of qualifications, Skirt with leaves, Where spirits are high, Bath seat, Won land, A zero is one.

More like this please…

commentBody:
I am so tickled by this puzzle and its setter!

And I won't do my typical Weintraubian fanboi thing 'cause Ms. Hood already KINDASORTAMAYBE did it in her comments.

Nah.  She did do it for reals.

Just smile after smile after smile for me as my 5 sad and far-flung across fills slowly, patchily, fitfully coaxed the grid to surrender ever-darkening sections of solve and give up its light even as the sun rose here and inexorably vanquished the ever-roiled mid-east darkness.

I wonder whether the Deity sets the night sky as a puzzle for the Sun to solve every morning.

And what celestial, ineluctable and ever-changing clues can it be given from day to day in order to hold its interest?

Dare I say that the Venn diagram of crucigram pleasure, for me, skews powerfully in the direction of female setters?

As does my preference, I must say, for the overall social company I prefer to keep.

I really liked this one.  Not the toughest Saturday imho, but thoroughly enjoyable.  The paratha clues and the Bath seat were gimmes for me. I thought at first there was a rare mistake in the 62 across clue. Much of the other clueing went from huh? to ah!  Well done!

That was tough for me! But so rewarding to complete…. Please more Saturdays like this but not another OREO clue…. So tedious… how much do they pay NYT for this regular placement??? 
 As an aside, can we have a Billy Joel themed clue by way of an apology for the NYT shamefully omitting him from the top 30 American songwriters….

I've been a huge Katie Hoody fan since I first met one of her puzzles. Ditto Robyn Weintraub.

While cruising through this one and barely ever tapping the brakes, I was lamenting the lack of resistance I expect from Katie's puzzles. Not surprised to see it's Katie's first grid with Robyn's assistance; to me, it felt more Weintraubian. (I finished in about half the time Katie's puzzles usually take, and about 50% longer than Robyn's do.)

Katie/Robyn didn't disappoint today, but I do wish it had lasted a bit longer.

Good, honest battle for me.  Didn't love POKERPARTY but the puzzle had several great, shticky clues.  On another note for you crossword lovers, the weekend WSJ has some pretty interesting  intermittent puzzles and (nothing against my NYT paid subscription) it's free.  Just sayin'...

commentBody:
Okay I can't help it and I have to kvell:

Would someone please write a film script a la Thelma and Louise for Robyn and Katie based on a fateful trip to a crossword convention and its tragic aftermath?

There.

I hope I'm done fanboing for the day.

But it's early here...

Another tasty serving of misdirects, contradictions, and clever deals.

IMHO, Katie Hoody qualifies, (with no ifs, ands, or buts), for top chef honors with this great themeless thali.

Paneer, Aloo . . .all that's missing is a cool raita and papadum.

Now I'm all hungry!

I'll take my wordplay Extra Hot, for any of you spicy alchemists!

It's the weekend - celebrate a little joy.

My initial comment never posted. 
Normally wouldn’t care. 

But I needed to just show my appreciation once again (two days in a row) to the constructors and editors. It’s clear to me, at least, that great clueing trumps a great grid. 2026 has made this evident. 

Don’t get me wrong.  I love fun, innovative entries.  But if they are paired with thoughtless clues, it just kills the fun.  

I wonder how many of us would feel if the clueing these last two days were like the clues from Thursday.  I don’t think we would have the same appreciation for your grid.  

Thank you Katie (and Will et al)

It took all I had but I solved this without lookups, which makes the clue for 15A look questionable - I am apparently much more than a MERE (Polish) MORTAL 🤣

I thought all the things I didn't know would do me in - I mean... DABO?!?! - but I solved around and across them, ultimately, and educated guesses the like of LEO I helped, too.

I struggled so much I didn't enjoy the solve for most of its duration, but when everything finally fell into place, I did feel a certain satisfaction. I liked yesterday's puzzle more than this.

I tend to start a crossword by filling in only what I know (or am pretty sure of), leaving the ambiguous clues for later. But when most of the puzzle is ambiguous clueing, it can be hard to find a way in!

I got lucky with some early guesses like ARI SHAPIRO, YOURE EARLY and STEAK SALAD. But this took some effort and creative thinking, and that’s what made it fun. Lots of little aha moments, and awesome fill like KINDA SORTA MAYBE. Loved that. And now I’m really wishing I had a STRAWBERRY SHAKE.
My one and only association with DABO is the fictional casino game from Star Trek: Deep Space Nine. Sadly I did not win any gold-pressed latinum by solving today’s puzzle (╥﹏╥)

First seemed tough...  Then all of a sudden, it didn't. Always seemed fun! Finished faster than average, surprisingly.

Lots of really great clues. I'd say more specifically, but we're in a hotel and my seriously so amazing husband is sleeping, as normal people do at this hour, so I don't want to voice text and wake him, but I hate fat fingering it.

Down Milwaukee way for my beloved nibling's graduation from Marquette. So proud of her. Oh, the places she'll go!! ❤️
Proper Saturday.
Wow, good one. I had a lot filled wrong, changed, changed again. Good clues, good answers! Thanks!

KINDASORTAMAYBE a real hard decent Saturday 

Thanks. 

It was a real struggle for me. Not knowing ARISHAPIRO, DABO or how to order a STRAWBERRYSHAKE made hard to find an in. 

I did get a foothold with LIMA and KOREA and spent too long thinking that ‘fame’ ought not be bought. 

At the end a good start to the weekend
tough as nails loved it thanks Katie!  21m for a Saturday I'm thrilled and it wasn't easy keep Saturdays challenging
Nice one! Especially loved the long entries' clues, STRAWBERRYSHAKE, SCARYMOVIE, KINDASORTAMAYBE, STEAKSALAD were really witty.
10/10 Saturday. Also because it made me crave aloo paratha and paneer paratha.
Two crunchy puzzles in a row! Keep em coming!
I don't know how I got convinced that POULTRYBAR is a thing.
Who else thought of Mae West @ 21A?

I found the Mini to be a dazzling, dizzying display of cruciverbial craftiness. Thanks staff, played very well. https://youtu.be/UfC9J8vx95w?si=ZwXct7_hRAYCr7OY My little Honda was a ‘71, 350cc with custom z-bars. SIGNING Out! Bru

Brilliant cluing.

Kindasortamaybe, alchemists... nice!
wow this is the hardest solve in years!  fun!

Loved this puzzle!! Started with virtually nothing it came together somehow. Laughed out loud as some answers fell into place - like STRAWBERRY MILKSHAKE  and BABAR and ILLRACEYOU. 

But could somebody explain STEAK SALAD to me? How is it a skirt with leaves?
Fun puzzle
I kinda sorta LOVED  the clueing for alchemist, rooftop bar and steak salad.

There's a reason three of Katie Hoody's puzzles made my POY list last year.  Just look at those clues!

 [Portable retirement option] for such mundane fill as COT was exceptional, as was [Won land] for KOREA and [List of qualifications] for KINDA SORTA MAYBE, but really, I'd be here all day if I listed everything I liked about this puzzle.

Oddly, this took me about 22% longer than Friday's but, unlike yesterday, I never got that "Uh oh, is this the one that'll do me in feeling?".

Deliciously difficult! 

I had about 3 passes through before things started to come together. This is what a masterful Saturday looks like!! Difficult cluing, and no obscure names as answers. (Well, I’ve never heard of 37D, but I could get it from all of the crosses)
So brilliant and absolutely satisfying to solve. 3 minutes below my average, which I will happily take, given I had almost no words for the first half of the time!

This was a great Saturday puzzle! Challenging, but reasonable and fair.The NE was brutal. Confidently plopping in UMPS and IRA certainly didn't help. I put in and removed ARFS and STRATI before putting them back before rightfully binning UMPS and IRA in that section. BABAR and then ROOFTOPBAR finally put me on the path to success.

Tomorrow is going to be my "streak break" day, I've decided. No NYT games, let all the counters reset and ignore them in the future.

I think the main reason is a comment someone made a few days ago lamenting that they used to do crosswords on paper and time wasn't a thing at all. So true, and made me think of the many fond memories of spending ages with a juicy newspaper crossword (some UK weekend cryptic) and a friend or family member, and just feeling joy. The NYT setup encourages short-term, individual satisfaction purely for daily engagement. Totally opposite to how it should be done.

Streaks, timers, badges - be gone!!
Delightful puzzle.
What a great puzzle.
Nothing to bark about here.  Had to give some weighty thought to the NE corner, though.  Nice one.

Fabulous, fun, frolick.

Fer realz.

Fanx.

This is what I like from a Saturday. A bamboozling first pass with multiple red herrings, small footholds appearing and then a flood of realization as I got into the constructor's mindset. The trivia was transatlantic culture stuff I enjoyed checking after guessing from crosses (no straight lookups needed), just the tonic after yesterday's battle. There'll probably be the usual flexmoaning that this should have been a Monday or whatever, but I loved it.

STRAWBERRYSHAKE, ROOFTOPBAR, SCARYMOVIE, KOREA, BABAR - fun, ingenious, whimsical.

MEREMORTAL (!), STEAKSALAD, KINDASORTAMAYBE - the less said the better.

The highs are worth the lows though, you have to swing and miss sometimes to hit a few sixes.

Also - please tell me y'all across the ocean don't really use the word 'winningest'...
Sarah Sinclair and Eric Agard co-constucted a very nice crossword for the LA Times today.  If you need some more puzzle time, I highly recommend it.
More Katie! More Robyn!

I thought this would be the puzzle that broke me, but in the end, the “stare at it until it blinks” method worked for me, although it sure took a long time. One of the (many) things I loved about it was that in each of the four parts, when the wall finally started to crack, it crumbled quickly, but it contributed nothing to progress in the next quadrant, and you were faced with another equally tricky challenge.

This was the perfect level of challenge for me. Some real thinkers, but didn't have to look anything up. So many clues that made perfect sense only after I'd gotten enough crossing letters to set me in the right direction. Such a satisfying solve!

Wow, what a lot of fun -- challenging, and sometimes bamboozling, but extremely clever and fair. 

NEC was the slowest to come to me -- I was stuck for a while on ILLCALLYOU, IRA, and STRATA. 

Absolutely loved "List of qualifications" (didn't get it right away, not until about 25-30% of the fill, but then I almost laughed out loud) and "Parody of _____" (only needed three fill letters, the first visual that came to me was Courtney Cox, then the movie name, big smile). 

Five stars for this puzzle. (I agree with  Katie on ARFS -- that's probably the only one that would get this puzzle a 99 instead of 100).

This was a real tough one for me. But there's a lot to be said about doing it on paper and not worrying about time. I walked away from it several times to do other things. Each time I came back to it I saw new possibilities.

In the end, it was DABO that was the last to fall, and I only got it because after all the other surrounding answers, it could only have been BOAR. I had no idea what a razorback was and thought it was a clam, but I hadn't filled that in.

Even though I can appreciate the great cluing and being able to finish without having to throw in the towel or look anything up, I didn't get the terrific sense of accomplishment I got from yesterday's puzzle, and I wonder why. But I did get quite a chuckle from New YUK University, and its callback to MOE.
Great Friday and Saturday puzzles this week.
I had POT in for 32A. Was relieved I spotted the mistake when I saw the answer to 12D.

Great themeless from Katie Hoody!

I do agree with everyone saying it could have been swapped with yesterday’s*, but they really felt quite similar — tons of fun clueing, and minimal boilerplate fill (there was a little more of that in today’s, but IMHO the ratio of fun fill to junk fill was still quite high).

And while the long answers largely stole the show today, there were a handful of short entries with groan-tastic clueing that I really enjoyed, including BIRD, YORE, BABAR and CRAMPS.

(Personally, I finished today’s and yesterday’s in about the same time…but that’s only because it somehow took me 4-5 minutes at the very end today to realize how badly my hasty guess of “laker” in 19A had botched the crossing downs. Funnily, I actually had CRUX for 3D first, but overwrote the “X” with “laker” because I couldn’t immediately think of an NBA team with an “X” in that position. Should have caught that within my first 10 seconds of flyspecking, but alas, my eyes somehow kept skipping over “amyl”, “leoa”, and “cruk”. Thanks, Obama!)
the difficulty level has noticeably increased for me over the last two weeks and I couldn’t be happier! Fun puzz with interesting clues. Same Gary Larson as Far Side?

Well, yesterday I wished that today's puzzle would be even more difficult than the Friday offering. For me---wish granted. This one took 42 minutes ... seven more than yesterday's. In retrospect, perhaps it shouldn't have taken that long, but for some reason several of the long entries just didn't click today.

"Portable retirement option" for COT was brilliant. "Skirt with a bunch of leaves, perhaps" was certainly misdirecting. "Won land" for KOREA almost got me, but this must have been used before. "Weighty subject of some children's books" for BABAR was outstanding, as was "Statement that may be followed by a dash" for ILLRACEYOU. That said, "Gold meddlers?" for ALCHEMISTS went too far trying to be clever/cute, since at best alchemists meddled with base metals like lead in their attempt to produce gold. 

I'm a real fan of Ms. Hoody's grids, which are typically very interesting. Her constructions would be even better if she could free them of stuff like DABO and LEED. Doing so would take them to the next level ... IMHO.

Really good puzzle today, with especially fine cluing. Thanks!

Great Saturday puzzle.  "Finished" in 44 minutes, but the "Congrats" window did not appear.  Had to check line by line, clue by clue til stumbling on a typo (AISO vs ALSO).  Had hardly anything on the first swing through the clues, but 2, 3, 4, 5 & 6 D provided a good base for getting more fills.  Think more solves came from crossings than the actual clues.

I'm surprised by the folks saying this was hard, as i hit this at half my Saturday average, but at least this puzzle was palate-cleansing fun after yesterday's smarmy clever b.s. These punned clues were all nice misdirections, as opposed to just KINDA SORTA MAYBE pointing to phrases no one actually uses. (incidentally, KINDASORTAMAYBE was the highlight of this puzzle for me...very fun clue and fill.)

Like others definitely had UMPS and IRA as obvious (but incorrect) fills, and there were a few more I loved like KOREA. Thanks for not putting us through back to back slogs, NYT.
Fun Saturday puzzle. I despaired first time through. Had it not been for my love of Indian cuisine, I’d have been lost.
I loved ARFS.
I missed the theme, but the clever clues made up for it. Great puzzle! 1 hour even.

One of the SLYEST puzzles ever, misdirections out the ying-yang. I loved it.

As for 67A --- are skirt steaks called that outside the US?

Julius Erving, "Dr. J", spent his entire *NBA* career with the Philadelphia Seventy-SIXERs, but every kid who grew up on Long Island in the '70s knows that he started his professional career with the New York Nets in the *ABA* with its red, white, and blue blue balls and three-point shots.

Joining the NBA put terrible financial strain on the Nets, and they compensated by selling Dr. J to the SIXERS,... 
and they went from being a championship team to....

Call it the "curse of the Good Doctor".

I had CURRICULUMVITAE for 38A [List of qualifications?]. It had the right number of letters but didn't throw me off for too long.

I should have known AMYS Kitchen because I've bought it. I never knew Dr. J. but after looking him up, I figured out SIXER. Nice seeing BABAR which I liked as a child. ARFS didn't bother me. How else do huskies say hello?
Fun one! Fingers crossed that this week's Friday and Saturday puzzles signify a return to (challenging) form 🤞

Extremely imaginative clues -- some that occasionally verged on unfair, but never in a way that made me cross.  My favorites, all of them fair but highly unusual, were KINDA SORTA MAYBE; YOU'RE EARLY; I'LL RACE YOU; SCARY MOVIE; MAKE-UP GAME; and STEAK SALAD.  For the latter: Admit it --you were thinking of an island hula dancer, weren't you?  And KOREA -- Won must be a place in KOREA, right?  That is SO good!!!

I also love MERE MORTAL.  That's putting "you and me" in our place, right?

So now for the bordering on unfair ones, not that I especially minded:  ALCHEMISTS "meddle" with the metal BEFORE it becomes gold -- in order to turn it INTO gold.  Once it IS gold, they stop meddling and leave it the heck alone.  That's what I say.

And ALSO (40D) seems like a stretch to me.  Like everyone else, I was looking for the tee or whatever you put the football on when kicking an extra point.  When ALSO came in, I didn't say "Aha!" -- what I said was: "One word does not a 'set-up' make."

But mostly a real gem of a themeless with a lot of thought put into pretty much every clue.  Fun to solve.
This was wonderful! Wrecked my average...but worth it! Well done

Although pickleball has gained significant ground as a newfound religion, SEC football is still king in the south. The names of some of the coaches befit the southern vernacular, almost as though the name just screams "football coach." When faced with a choice between a name like, let's say, Gregory Quahog (just made that up) or Dabo Swinney, I'm going with the Dabo guy. It just has that iconic bubba-style name.
A few of SEC football's other down-south names:
Kirby Smart (Go Dawgs)
Urban Meyer
Lane Kiffin
Kalen DeBoer

See what I mean?

I liked this one a lot!  It took a while for it to come together, and there were so many little "aha" moments.  I thought the cluing was really clever and it had me chuckling all the way to the happy music.  Well done!

Nice puzzle. Typical long Saturday workout for me, with a lot of pondering and working the crosses. But that just made for more than a few nice 'aha' moments when I finally had enough for an answer to dawn on me. And... one puzzle find today. A Sunday from March 30, 1969 by E. Toro with the title: "What's that again?" Some theme answers: HOOTCHIECOOTCHIE HOITYTOITY HODGEPODGE HANKYPANKY HOCUSPOCUS HOWDYDOODY HIGGLEDYPIGGLEDY HELTERSKELTER HURRYSCURRY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=3/30/1969&g=57&d=A ....
Of all dog breeds, a husky is least likely to greet you with an "arf."  They mostly vocalize with a whine/soft howling voice.

Who fell for every misdirect in the book?  Why, it's the MOL--feeling even OLDer!
I didn't realize ARI SHAPIRO had written a book.
How could I get a couple of long entries like WENT ON TOUR and KINDA SORTA MAYBE and yet be so, so stumped?
I wanted a fancier word for a SHAKE--FRAPPE wasn't fitting.
And haven't we been advised by our Friends Across the Pond that 39D is rude and crude?
No matter what, you can't make me go down the candy aisle!
That about covers it....Wow, Katie.  I mean, OW.
Poker Party....yeah, that's not a thing.
This goes on my puzzle list of favorites for sure.
Loved this puzzle! Was very fun to solve. I always enjoy Robyn Weintraub’s work so it’s great to find out in the Constructor Notes her influence and involvement :)

Fun, crunchy puzzle. Two in a row! We could argue whether this was harder or easier than yesterday's; it's gonna be a YMMV. While I was constantly sweating during today's solve, I never felt like I was going to be defeated by the puzzle, as I did yesterday, so... But this is not a complaint. Lots of things to love in this puzzle, many have been pointed out already: "List of qualifications", "high spirits", "followed by a dash", to name a few. But I want to reserve a special shout out to Leslie Nielsen and the little "New YUK University of Knuckleheads"! Did you notice the acronym of this institution of higher laughter? NYUK, NYUK, NYUK!!! MOE gets the first mention (and is a welcome gimme), but Curly gets the last laugh! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KZSyGCIBDEs Thanks, Katie Hoody!

Quite a broad cultural spectrum in today's puzzle, ranging from an NPR host to a college football coach. And nice to see DABO Swinney make an appearance. Good Lord, how many times have we had ARA Parseghian?

Loved this one; a pleasure to solve, with so many cleverly oblique clues ("Kilo follower" and "Setup for an extra point," among others). Went through half the across clues, baffled, before I solved one. Wound up completing in 28 minutes with only a couple of Google cheats:).
At first I thought there had to have been a mistake in 62-across; then I marveled at the recursiveness of the clue.
Brava, Katie Hoody!

Some fun clues in there, perfect challenge level. Thank you!

It took a little alchemy to attain the gold star today, but it is in hand after 36:23, every second enjoyable!

CAIROMAINE "Former Russian crucifer?" LENINGRADISH And some other theme answers: OTTAWATERMELON LONDONION QUITOMATO TOKYOKRA CARACASPARAGUS HELINKIWI BEIRUTABAGA Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=12/13/1992&g=107&d=A ....

I dunno, I enjoyed ARFS! And maybe because we just had it in the singular a few days ago (Wednesday, May 6), it was a rare early toehold in this puzzle generally, and the NE especially. Crossing it with DOG SITTERS took it from casual crossword fill to adorable! Though if you really want a nit, I didn't love the pluralization of 14D, lol. 

It might be sacrilegious to say, but after a hectic day yesterday, I dozed off in the last few minutes of the Knicks-SIXERs game last night(!) — once it was clear Brunson had pulled us ahead by a comfortable margin... again... 

That said, it was wonderful to see the good Dr. in the grid, even more so after yesterday's [All slam dunks]. He was *all* the slam dunks – and so much more – during his career!! 

This puzzle was full of high spirits, sparkling references and misdirect hijinks. Loved it! Thanks Katie!!
I went from annoyed and frustrated with the clueing early on to increasingly amused and appreciative.  This was one of the most clever and satisfying fills I’ve done in a long time.
Indeed it was a treat and gone too soon. Now we await next Friday puzzle. Unless the editors give us a nice, controversial 1000 comments rebus on Thursday. Amen.
I considered cheating, but I didn’t want to bother!  Then from chaos came order, in under 26:00.  Extra satisfying when that happens.

A wonderful Saturday that nearly kicked my 39 Down. Katie Hoody has an impressive body of work thus far, on her own and in collaboration with her husband. How perfect to read in her notes that she has reached out to Robyn Weintraub, both personal favorites and very special constructors IMHO.

It was so enjoyable to finish a puzzle with such delighted  feelings of both accomplishment and entertainment. I don’t always follow constructors by name or recognize them for past submissions, but I will have to remember Katie Hoody. 
Without reading every entry here, I already suspect that most of the clues have been highlighted. But let me add my respect for 62A’s meta cluing and 67A’s skirt with leaves. Some clues like 52D’s Won may have been used before (I think?) but still caught me off guard. And even though I’d be happy to never see OREO again, the clue for 33D had me thinking of newspapers, so that felt like a nice change. 
Meanwhile, I have to confess to the embarrassment of momentarily wondering who ARISHA PIRO is. But figured I’d give you all the same chuckle I had. (As if I haven’t been listening to NPR for thirty years…)
Last, for those discussing their times this week: I was seven minutes over my average for Friday and 10 minutes under today. But this Saturday puzzle was far too clever to be dismissed as easy.
It solved pretty quickly, but I loved how clever a lot of the clues were. Loved the self-referential 62a 😊
Some really spectacular cluing here. Humbling but fair.

Oh oh, it's always fun. As a former proofreader, I'm always on the hunt for typos. I found one in the Wordplay column. Can you find it?

"37D. This is the first time this particular trivia has been in the Crossword; it is a little obscure, in my opinion, and impossible to deduce. [Clemson’s ____ Swinney (winningest coach in A.C.C. history] is a reference to DABO Swinney."
33A hung me up for a little bit, until I remembered the number one rule of crosswords: When in doubt, OREO.

You know it's a good puzzle when you have multiple *click - aHA!* moments, and this one was full of those. Crunchy, fair, brilliant cluing, and I feel smarter after completing it. Magnificent construction, Kate!
Loved it! Thank you. Yes, I was fairly sure that 62A was a typo and I just knew the radio interview was Terry Gross. An excellent puzzle.
The NW corner was an instant gimme. “What day is this? It’s record time!” But that confidence faded quickly. Only a few minutes faster than my average thanks to lots of clever clues. Three thumbs up.

Loved this gem.  Clever, funny, and just hard enough to feel like an accomplishment when the last corner (NE, for me) clicked into place.  And it was nice to get my confidence back after Friday!

I eagerly await the next grid with a Katie Hoody byline!

DABO could have been clued with a Star Trek reference. 

Love those DABO girls!

I loved so many things about this puzzle, everything other commenters are saying. 

Was a tiny bit worried when I got the (delightful) 1A immediately, but by the time I got down to the clue for the Greek letter, I knew we were in good hands. This constructor (and/or editor) knows how to under-specify something. You know you'll get it on crosses; you know you have no hope of getting it right now.

Laughed out loud when the list of qualifications emerged from a few crosses.

On Saturdays, I'm usually disappointed in overly direct clues, but today, because of the ingenious answer of so many of them, the few direct ones (say, for YETI and TALK) felt more like gentle jokes. 

My solve was a little quicker than usual, but the richness made it a satisfying meal. More, more, Katie!
Superb Saturday puzzle. So many well-crafted clues. The BABAR one, coincidentally matching "death" as a weighty subject, was tops for me.  Nice job!

I EASILY got 6-down "One of the Three Stooges." 3 letters. 
I am happy to offer assistance in solving this one for anyone who wishes to request it, as it does call upon a knowledge base in which I am quite confident. 
I didn't really get any of the other answers in the crossword, but this one I effin' NAILED so just reach out. I got you, fam. 
I did start the puzzle confident I was gonna rip it up, lots of feral energy, but the clues were so hard I just ended up an uncertain razorback.

Hard for me but fun! 4 word-checks and some look ups. I looked up the winningest coach and took a surprisingly long time to come up with OREO. A clever clue. I was thinking of Charlie Chaplin. My two favorites were:

1) ALCHEMIST for Gold meddlers as first I thought miners, then sports. But which sport? 

2) KINDA SORTA MAYBE for “list of qualifications”.

And 3) I thought ARO SHAPIRO was ARISHA PIRO: just a name I didn’t know. 

Wonderful job, Katie Hoody!
Very good puzzle. As others have said not too hard, not too easy. I loved kindasortamaybe. Finished just a few minutes above average
Hobo spiders last time and an all time favorite "weighty subject" this time. Katie, what a unique and interesting child you must have been. This was a gem. Thanks.

This was a great Saturday puzzle. 

Thank you, Katie!

To me, this represents the quintessential Saturday puzzle. Like Caitlin's first pass, mine yielded very little, but with patience and a good bit of coffee, I wended my way through delightful misdirects, clever iterations of the usual subjects (I'm looking at YOU, Oreo; I first entered "film" there), and arrived at the pot of gold (star) in under half an hour. 

I will add, just to emphasize the pleasure the puzzle brought me, that it far outpaced any of the feelings I experienced helping my spouse spread mulch for an hour in a light drizzle! 🤣
Excellent puzzle. Lots of clever clues. Made me chuckle several times. Well done!

Absolutely fantastic Saturday challenge.  No look-ups, but after combing through the clues twice and still staring at a vast wall of white squares with precious few toeholds, I did invite collaboration over coffee with Sports Fan Husband and Very Clever Daughter. The former reminded me that Dr J was a SIXER instead of a LAKER; knew the Kings of LA were not movie stars but a hockey TEAM; and rattled off DABO Swinney like it was his best friend's name. Clever daughter immediately saw through the misdirect at 32A and corrected IRA to COT; she also loved SCARYMOVIE, which I never have never seen because I dislike scary movies.  I am a MEREMORTAL, but with my family's help I KINDASORTAMAYBE muddled through.  Great puzzle.

Such as ARI SHAPIRO’s name, the fact that Dr. J was a SIXER, that there is a certification program called LEED, and that the PAWNEE nation has the named council. With those 4 clearing roadblocks, the rest was thoroughly enjoyable!

I think this puzzle just doubled my average time over many years ... but seriously, my solve took a good deal longer than usual for Saturday, because I found many clues to be epitomes of lexical deceit!

Was not sure why KOREA is "Won land", but this could be due to my misspent youth in which I incomprehensibly disdained my history lessons. "Postponed court date" could have been innumerable things besides MAKEUP GAME. The meta clue for SCARY MOVIE was inscrutable to me at first. "List of qualifications" for KINDA SORTA MAYBE should win a prize for being a great clue (not to mention a great answer to appear in any crossword). "Gold meddlers?" for ALCHEMISTS is another great clue. The most difficult one of all these clues, I found to be *really tough* — in no small part thanks to the obdurate crossing words — was "Skirt with many leaves, perhaps", sending my mind to Tahiti.

Tahiti may have been off course, but all in all I loved the wild ride that this puzzle sent me on!

When I taught chemistry at a university, I heard students talking about how they had to memorize the periodic chart in high school chemistry.

I thought that was appalling.  THE one thing a chemist counts on having at hand is the periodic chart.  

(Having said that, I did memorize it--including the atomic numbers and atomic weights, but I did that so when I was doing examples at the board I wouldn't have to step away to find an atomic weight.  When I went running I'd have a stack of flash cards, so I memorized it all on the run.  I don't know it any more.)

Anyway, I rarely had things I expected them to memorize.  The ALCHEMISTs had to memorize a lot, but that was because that was prior to understanding.  Understanding should eliminate some memorization.

@Alex streaks contrive obligation, and app-makers et al exploit that strategically. Always a relief to lose one, if nothing else because usually life got in the way

@Alex Maybe the crossword “should” be done however the person doing it enjoys doing it. Time isn’t a thing for me, and the NYT doesn’t make it so for me. I just do the puzzles as I do them. But it’s always great to take a break from any routine that starts to grate. Enjoy your free day!
@Alex There is a nice scene in Acapulco(streaming comedy series set in 1985-1986) where a couple is doing a crossword together with pencil and paper and it’s very sweet.

@Alex - I agree with Theresa's comment that crosswords "should" be done however one likes. Unless you're in an actual tournament, you're competing against no one but yourself, and you can ignore times, streaks, and all the other bells and whistles of the digital age if you choose to do so.

As for me, I never got into solving crosswords on paper, and still have no interest in doing so. But since I started solving the NYT crossword online just over four years ago, it has become one of my favorite hobbies. To each their own.
